step.title: Step 99
step.actionDisplay: MOUSE(row=1, col=31) -> MOUSE(row=7, col=31) -> MOUSE(row=31, col=49) -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=1, col=31), MOUSE(row=7, col=31), MOUSE(row=31, col=49).
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 398, level 2.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 323 actions; a strong score needs about 48 or fewer. You are 6.7x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 32 actions returned the board to a state already seen 32 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 32 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
REVISIT WASTE: this board state has recurred 6 times recently — you are cycling back through states you have already visited. Break the loop and commit to a new line of play.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so the game is over again after clicking on the right green block (31, 49). The pattern is clear:
- In the new state, clicking on the top 2 green blocks is OK, but clicking on the right green block causes game over.
- Clicking on the middle row (4 blocks) and bottom row (4 blocks) is OK.
So the right green block (31, 49) is a "trap" or "blocker" that causes game over when clicked. The goal in the new state is to click on all green blocks EXCEPT the right one.
But wait, in the new state, there are 11 green blocks:
- Top: 2 blocks
- Right: 1 block (trap)
- Middle row: 4 blocks
- Bottom row: 4 blocks
That's 2 + 1 + 4 + 4 = 11 blocks. If I click on all 10 non-trap blocks, the level should complete.
Let me try this: reset the game, execute the sequence to reach the new state, and then click on all 10 non-trap green blocks (top 2, middle 4, bottom 4), skipping the right one.
Actually, wait. Let me think about the order. In the initial state, the correct sequence was: click on top row (7 blocks), move RIGHT to end, click on bottom row from right to left (7 blocks). In the new state, maybe the correct sequence is: click on top (2 blocks), move player, click on middle (4 blocks), move player, click on bottom (4 blocks), skipping the right one.
But I don't know if moving the player is necessary in the new state. Let me try clicking on all 10 non-trap blocks without moving the player, and see if the level completes.
Actually, I just realized something. In the new state, the player is at (37, 50), which is on the right side of the board. The right green block is at (31, 49), which is near the player. Maybe the player needs to move to a different position before clicking on the green blocks.
But in the initial state, the player started at (37, 20), and the correct sequence was to click on the top row, move RIGHT to the end, and then click on the bottom row. The player's position didn't matter for clicking on the green blocks; it only mattered for moving RIGHT.
Let me just try clicking on all 10 non-trap blocks in the new state, and see if the level completes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Reset game over
action(['ACTION7'])

# Execute the sequence to reach the new state
top_row = 25
bottom_row = 31
cols = [13, 19, 25, 31, 37, 43, 49]

# Click on top row
for c in cols:
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': top_row, 'col': c}])

# Move RIGHT to end
for i in range(5):
    action(['RIGHT'])

# Click on bottom row from right to left
for c in reversed(cols):
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': bottom_row, 'col': c}])

# Now in the new state, click on all 10 non-trap green blocks
# Top: (1, 31), (7, 31)
# Middle: (37, 13), (37, 19), (37, 25), (37, 31)
# Bottom: (43, 13), (43, 19), (43, 25), (43, 31)
# Skip: (31, 49) - the right green block (trap)

non_trap = [(1, 31), (7, 31), (37, 13), (37, 19), (37, 25), (37, 31), (43, 13), (43, 19), (43, 25), (43, 31)]

for i, (r, c) in enumerate(non_trap):
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': r, 'col': c}])
    result = last_action_result
    print(f"Click ({r},{c}): game_over={result.get('game_over', False)}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed', False)}, done={result.get('done', False)}")
    if result.get('game_over', False) or result.get('level_completed', False) or result.get('done', False):
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
